heroOverview: A liver hemangioma is a benign vascular malformation made up of a cluster of enlarged blood vessels within the liver parenchyma — the most common benign liver tumour, found incidentally in 1-5% of all abdominal imaging studies. The great majority are solitary, measure under 3 cm, and require no treatment beyond one confirmatory imaging follow-up. The name comes from haema (blood) and angioma (vessel tumour), which accurately describes its pathology: a sponge-like tangle of blood-filled endothelial channels. Unlike hepatocellular carcinoma or metastatic disease, a hemangioma is not cancerous, does not turn cancerous, and does not affect liver function. Most people live their entire lives without knowing they have one. When symptoms occur — usually from lesions exceeding 4-5 cm — they take the form of right upper abdominal fullness or discomfort. Accurate diagnosis on MRI or contrast-enhanced ultrasound almost always avoids the need for biopsy.

keyStats.prevalence: Found in 1-5% of the general population on imaging; autopsy series suggest up to 7%; the most common benign liver tumour
keyStats.demographics: More common in women than men (roughly 2-5:1 ratio); incidence peaks between ages 30-50
keyStats.avgAge: Most often diagnosed between ages 30-50, frequently as an incidental finding on abdominal ultrasound
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300-500 million people worldwide carry a liver hemangioma, given a population prevalence of 4-7%

definition: A liver hemangioma (ICD-10: D18.09 — hemangioma of other sites; relevant also under hepatic lesion classifications) is a hamartomatous malformation of the hepatic vasculature in which endothelial-lined vascular channels of varying calibre form a discrete, well-demarcated mass within the liver parenchyma. Histologically the most common subtype is the cavernous hemangioma, characterised by large, thin-walled blood-filled sinusoids separated by fibrous septa. Capillary hemangiomas are smaller and rarer. Giant hemangiomas are arbitrarily defined as those exceeding 4 cm (some authorities use 5 cm); they are more likely to produce symptoms, undergo sclerotic change, or cause the rare complication of spontaneous rupture. The lesion arises from congenital vascular dysplasia rather than neoplastic proliferation, and mitotic activity is absent. Oestrogen is thought to promote growth, explaining why giant hemangiomas are far more frequent in women and why lesions may enlarge during pregnancy or oral contraceptive use. Liver function tests, alpha-fetoprotein, and other tumour markers remain normal unless a co-existing liver disease is present. The condition falls under hepatology and interventional radiology when active management is needed; the vast majority are managed expectantly by the initial detecting clinician.

typesClassification.description: The most common form: a well-circumscribed sponge of enlarged vascular channels, usually under 3 cm, with the classic peripheral nodular enhancement pattern on contrast CT or MRI. Requires only one confirmatory scan.

Arbitrarily defined as greater than 4-5 cm. More likely to cause right upper quadrant discomfort, undergo central fibrosis or sclerosis, and — very rarely — rupture. May be heterogeneous on imaging due to internal scarring.

A small lesion under 1.5 cm that fills rapidly and homogeneously with contrast on arterial-phase imaging, mimicking a hypervascular tumour. MRI with hepatobiliary contrast usually resolves the distinction.

A hemangioma that has undergone thrombosis and fibrous replacement over time, producing an atypical appearance on imaging that may mimic a metastasis and occasionally requires biopsy for definitive diagnosis.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant or epigastric fullness or a dull, pressure-like ache, typically seen only with lesions larger than 4-5 cm pressing on adjacent structures.
Early satiety or a sensation that the stomach fills quickly during meals, caused by a large hemangioma exerting mass effect on the stomach or duodenum.
Nausea without vomiting, particularly after meals, when a large lesion displaces the gastric outlet.
Palpable abdominal mass in the right upper quadrant — uncommon, limited to very large or superficially positioned hemangiomas.
Referred right shoulder tip pain from diaphragmatic irritation by a large superior-surface hemangioma.
Abdominal bloating or a sense of heaviness in the right side, which is often positional and worsens after eating.
Acute severe right upper quadrant pain — rare, signalling spontaneous haemorrhage into the lesion or, extremely rarely, rupture into the peritoneum.
Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon: thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, and consumptive coagulopathy in giant lesions (extremely rare, mainly in children).
Obstructive jaundice — very rare, from a large hilar lesion compressing a major bile duct.
Incidental finding with no symptoms at all — the presentation in the majority of cases discovered during imaging for an unrelated condition.
earlyWarningSigns: A vague sense of right-sided upper abdominal heaviness appearing weeks before frank discomfort.
Mild early satiety that develops without a clear dietary change.
An unexplained right upper quadrant abnormality found on ultrasound ordered for another reason.
Subtle discomfort that worsens with vigorous physical activity in an individual known to have a hepatic lesion.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe right upper quadrant or diffuse abdominal pain with haemodynamic instability — may indicate spontaneous rupture with haemoperitoneum.
Rapidly dropping haemoglobin or haematocrit with abdominal pain after abdominal trauma, suggesting traumatic rupture of a subcapsular hemangioma.
Progressive jaundice with abdominal pain, which can indicate biliary compression by a rapidly enlarging giant hemangioma.
Signs of consumption coagulopathy — unexplained bruising, bleeding gums, petechiae — in the setting of a known giant hemangioma.

causes.description: Liver hemangiomas are considered hamartomas that arise from a developmental aberration of hepatic vascular channels in the embryo. They are present from birth, though most remain too small to detect until adulthood; no acquired cause has been identified for typical lesions.

Endogenous and exogenous oestrogen (oral contraceptives, hormone replacement therapy) promotes endothelial proliferation and enlargement of existing hemangiomas. This mechanism explains the 2-5-fold female predominance and reports of lesion growth during pregnancy.

Increased circulating blood volume and elevated oestrogen during pregnancy can cause a previously stable hemangioma to enlarge by 10-25% in some women, occasionally precipitating symptoms for the first time in the third trimester.

Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and other angiogenic mediators expressed locally within the lesion sustain its vascular channels. Higher levels of these factors are found in larger lesions, though no systemic abnormality of angiogenic signalling is established.

Rare familial clustering and the association of multiple hepatic hemangiomas with hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome) suggest a genetic component in a subset of cases, though most hemangiomas are sporadic.

riskFactors.description: Women are 2-5 times more likely than men to be diagnosed with a liver hemangioma, with the gap widest for giant lesions. The association is attributed to higher lifetime oestrogen exposure.
Long-term use of oestrogen-containing contraceptives has been linked to an increased likelihood of hemangioma enlargement; withdrawal generally stabilises the lesion, though it rarely reverses it.
Multiple pregnancies, particularly with high cumulative oestrogen exposure, are associated with larger hemangiomas and a higher rate of growth-related symptoms.
Postmenopausal women receiving oestrogen-containing HRT have been shown in small series to have higher rates of hemangioma growth compared with non-users; the risk is generally modest.

This autosomal dominant disorder of vascular dysplasia (mutations in ENG, ACVRL1) leads to multiple hepatic vascular malformations, including hemangiomas, arteriovenous malformations, and telangiectasias throughout the liver.
Hemangiomas are most often detected in middle-aged adults, likely because this is the age group most commonly undergoing abdominal imaging rather than because the lesions develop at this age.

riskFactors.factor: Previous abdominal imaging for other reasons
riskFactors.description: The rising use of ultrasound, CT, and MRI for unrelated indications has substantially increased the rate of incidental detection; access to imaging is therefore the strongest predictor of diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 30-50, the most commonly diagnosed group
Individuals of any sex undergoing abdominal imaging for unrelated gastrointestinal or urological symptoms
Women using long-term oral contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy
Pregnant women, in whom previously silent hemangiomas may become symptomatic if they enlarge
People with hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia, in whom multiple lesions are common
Adults over 60, in whom sclerosing hemangiomas are more often an incidental autopsy finding
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is almost always achieved non-invasively with cross-sectional imaging, and biopsy is avoided because it adds no benefit in typical cases and carries a small risk of haemorrhage. When a hepatic lesion is found on ultrasound, the classic appearance of a well-defined, hyperechoic lesion with posterior acoustic enhancement is suggestive but not specific. Multiphasic contrast-enhanced MRI is the gold-standard confirmatory test, offering sensitivity and specificity above 95% for hemangioma when the pathognomonic peripheral nodular enhancement with progressive centripetal fill-in is demonstrated. Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) with sulphur hexafluoride microbubbles can confirm the diagnosis at the same sitting as the initial ultrasound without radiation, making it an attractive first-line confirmatory tool. Contrast-enhanced CT (four-phase hepatic CT) is widely used and shows the same peripheral enhancement pattern, though with lower sensitivity for lesions under 1.5 cm. The main diagnostic challenge is the atypical or sclerosing hemangioma, which may lack classic enhancement and mimic metastatic disease; in oncology patients with atypical lesions, MRI with hepatobiliary agent (gadoxetate disodium) or ultimately a biopsy with immunohistochemistry for endothelial markers (CD31, CD34) resolves the question. Liver function tests, AFP, CEA, and CA 19-9 are checked to exclude coincident hepatocellular carcinoma or metastases rather than to diagnose hemangioma.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging that detects hemangioma as a well-defined hyperechoic lesion; highly sensitive for lesions above 1 cm, though not specific enough for definitive diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the abdomen and a handheld probe scans the liver in multiple planes; the test takes 15-20 minutes and requires no radiation or contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard confirmatory test achieving over 95% sensitivity and specificity, demonstrating the pathognomonic peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in on gadolinium-enhanced sequences.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in a narrow MRI tube for 30-45 minutes; a gadolinium contrast agent is injected through an arm vein mid-scan to capture enhancement patterns across arterial, portal, and delayed phases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the hemangioma diagnosis using sulphur hexafluoride microbubble contrast at the initial ultrasound visit; avoids radiation and provides real-time enhancement characterisation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An intravenous microbubble agent is injected during the ultrasound examination; the enhancement sequence is watched in real time and recorded for review, adding about 10 minutes to a standard scan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Widely available multiphasic CT that demonstrates peripheral enhancement and progressive fill-in; used when MRI is unavailable or contraindicated (e.g., pacemaker, claustrophobia).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie on a table that moves through a doughnut-shaped scanner; iodinated contrast is injected through a vein, and four sequential scans are acquired over about 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (liver function panel, AFP, CEA, CA 19-9)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hepatocellular carcinoma, metastatic disease, and co-existing liver pathology; results are normal in isolated hemangioma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results are available within a few hours to one business day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical lesions in oncology patients when imaging cannot confidently distinguish hemangioma from metastasis; CD31 and CD34 immunostaining confirm endothelial origin.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under ultrasound or CT guidance, a thin needle is passed through the skin into the liver under local anaesthesia; you rest in hospital for 4-6 hours for observation afterwards.

treatmentOverview: The large majority of liver hemangiomas require no treatment. For asymptomatic lesions under 5 cm with typical imaging appearances, the standard approach is one confirmatory imaging study (MRI or CEUS) followed by no further surveillance in the absence of symptoms or risk factors for growth. No medication has been shown to reliably reduce hemangioma size in adults, though mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, sirolimus) are used in the rare paediatric Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon. Oestrogen-containing medications (combined oral contraceptives, HRT) are typically discontinued or switched to progesterone-only or non-hormonal alternatives in women with giant or symptomatic hemangiomas, because withdrawing oestrogen may stabilise growth. Surgical resection (hepatic enucleation or anatomical resection) is reserved for a minority: those with disabling symptoms not explained by other pathology, a giant hemangioma with documented progressive enlargement, diagnostic uncertainty that persists after full imaging workup, or — rarely — acute rupture. Minimally invasive alternatives include transarterial embolisation (TAE), which reduces blood supply to the lesion and can shrink large symptomatic hemangiomas, and radiofrequency or microwave ablation for smaller lesions not amenable to enucleation. Liver transplantation is a last resort for exceptionally large multiple hemangiomas occupying most of the liver in highly symptomatic patients. The key principle is avoiding unnecessary intervention in a benign condition where the risk of operative morbidity generally exceeds the risk of the hemangioma itself.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation and watchful waiting
medicalTreatments.description: The mainstay for asymptomatic hemangiomas under 5 cm. One confirmatory MRI or CEUS scan is obtained to establish the diagnosis; no further imaging is needed unless symptoms develop or clinical concern arises. No medication is required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate for approximately 90-95% of diagnosed hemangiomas; the natural history is stable in over 90% of cases with no enlargement over 5-year follow-up.

medicalTreatments.name: Withdrawal of oestrogen-containing medications
medicalTreatments.description: In women with growing or symptomatic hemangiomas using combined oral contraceptives or oestrogen-containing HRT, switching to a non-oestrogen alternative is recommended. Cessation often stabilises the lesion without operative intervention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lesion growth arrested in approximately 70-80% of cases after oestrogen withdrawal, though significant regression is uncommon.

medicalTreatments.name: mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, sirolimus)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for the Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon — the rare consumptive coagulopathy associated with giant hepatic hemangiomas, predominantly in children. These agents reduce angiogenesis and lesion vascularity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Platelet count improvements observed in over 60% of paediatric Kasabach-Merritt cases in published case series; adult experience is limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Transarterial embolisation (TAE)
medicalTreatments.description: Catheter-directed embolisation of the arterial supply to a large symptomatic hemangioma, reducing its blood content and size. Performed by an interventional radiologist under conscious sedation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement reported in 70-85% of patients in published series, with lesion volume reduction of 30-60% at 6-12 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatic enucleation
surgicalOptions.description: The preferred surgical approach for isolated giant hemangiomas: the lesion is shelled out along the fibrous pseudocapsule plane without sacrificing normal hepatic parenchyma. Can be performed open or laparoscopically in selected centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom resolution in over 90% of surgically treated patients; post-operative complication rates of 10-20% in published series, including bile leak and haemorrhage.

surgicalOptions.name: Anatomical hepatic resection
surgicalOptions.description: Segmental or lobar liver resection when enucleation is not feasible due to central location or involvement of major vessels; associated with greater blood loss than enucleation but similar long-term outcomes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom control in approximately 85-95% of cases; used in fewer than 5% of all hemangioma diagnoses.

surgicalOptions.name: Radiofrequency or microwave ablation (RFA/MWA)
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous thermal ablation for symptomatic hemangiomas under 5 cm that are not amenable to resection or embolisation; destroys the vascular channels with heat.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete ablation achieved in 80-90% of lesions under 5 cm; recurrence rates of approximately 10-15% at 2 years in published series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary and lifestyle modification
alternativeTreatments.description: No specific diet reduces hemangioma size. However, avoiding phytoestrogen-heavy supplements and soy isoflavone preparations in women with large hemangiomas is sometimes advised, given the oestrogen-dependent growth mechanism, though evidence is anecdotal.

alternativeTreatments.name: Activity modification
alternativeTreatments.description: For giant hemangiomas exceeding 10 cm, some hepatologists advise avoiding high-impact contact sports or activities that risk blunt abdominal trauma, given the small but real risk of rupture. This is a precautionary recommendation rather than evidence-based.

whySeeSpecialist: An incidentally detected liver lesion should be reviewed by a hepatologist or liver-imaging specialist (usually a radiologist with hepatobiliary expertise) to confirm the diagnosis confidently. This matters because the management of a benign hemangioma — essentially reassurance and discharge — is entirely different from that of a malignant lesion. A specialist also identifies the minority of patients with giant or symptomatic hemangiomas who need an interventional or surgical consultation, and can advise on oestrogen-containing medications and monitoring during pregnancy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hepatologist or gastroenterologist with subspecialty interest in liver lesions, ideally in a centre that performs high-volume liver MRI and has an on-site interventional radiology team. For giant or symptomatic hemangiomas under consideration for treatment, a hepatobiliary surgeon experienced in enucleation is important. Ask how many liver hemangiomas the centre manages per year and whether CEUS is available as a radiation-free confirmatory option.
preventionStrategies: No intervention prevents a congenital liver hemangioma from forming.
Women with a known large hemangioma should discuss oestrogen-containing contraceptives with their clinician before starting or continuing them.
Pregnant women with a previously identified large hemangioma should have hepatology input early in pregnancy to plan monitoring.
Avoid unsupervised use of anabolic steroids or high-dose phytoestrogen supplements, which may promote vascular lesion growth.
Report any new or worsening right upper quadrant symptoms promptly if a liver lesion is known.
lifestyleModifications: If you have a giant hemangioma (over 5 cm), discuss with your clinician whether to avoid contact sports or activities that risk blunt abdominal trauma.
Maintain a healthy weight; severe obesity complicates hepatic imaging and surgery if intervention is ever needed.
Do not self-medicate with herbal oestrogen preparations or phytoestrogen supplements without medical advice.
Attend all imaging follow-up appointments scheduled by your specialist.
Keep your medical team informed of any change in abdominal symptoms, especially new or sudden pain.

recommended: A balanced diet supporting a healthy liver: plenty of vegetables, whole grains, lean protein, and moderate healthy fats
Adequate hydration to support liver perfusion
Coffee in moderate amounts, which has established hepatoprotective effects in general liver health (not specific to hemangioma)
avoid: High-dose phytoestrogen supplements (red clover, soy isoflavones) in women with large hemangiomas, given the oestrogen-dependent growth mechanism
Excessive alcohol, which has no direct link to hemangioma but impairs overall liver health and complicates any future surgery
Anabolic steroids and androgenic-anabolic supplements, which have been linked in case reports to vascular liver lesion growth

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is safe and recommended for overall health in people with liver hemangiomas. No exercise restriction is needed for lesions under 5 cm. For giant hemangiomas over 10 cm, high-impact contact sports and activities with a significant risk of abdominal trauma — such as boxing, rugby, or rodeo — are generally discouraged as a precaution. Swimming, cycling, yoga, and low-impact cardio carry no meaningful risk and are actively encouraged.
dailyManagement: Be aware of how your abdomen normally feels so you can recognise any new or worsening pain early.
If you take oestrogen-containing medication, ensure your prescribing clinician knows about your hemangioma and has assessed whether a switch to a non-oestrogen option is appropriate.
Keep a record of any follow-up imaging appointments and attend them as scheduled.
Seek same-day medical attention for any sudden severe abdominal pain — even though rupture is rare, it is a surgical emergency.
Inform any new doctor or surgeon of your liver hemangioma before any planned abdominal operation.
prognosis: The prognosis for a liver hemangioma is excellent. The lesion is benign, has zero malignant potential, and does not affect liver function. More than 90% of hemangiomas remain stable in size over decades of follow-up. A small proportion — estimated at under 10-20% — enlarge over time, and giant lesions in women of reproductive age are most prone to growth. Spontaneous rupture is reported in fewer than 1% of cases and is associated almost exclusively with lesions exceeding 10 cm or those subjected to abdominal trauma. When surgical treatment is necessary, outcomes are excellent: enucleation resolves symptoms in over 90% of patients with minimal long-term morbidity. The overall life expectancy of someone with a liver hemangioma is the same as the general population.
recoveryTimeline: For the majority of patients who require only watchful waiting, there is no recovery phase — the condition is managed with a single confirmatory imaging study and routine clinical follow-up. After hepatic enucleation or resection, hospital stay typically runs 3-7 days, with return to light activity in 2-3 weeks and full activity in 6-8 weeks. After transarterial embolisation, most patients are discharged within 24-48 hours; post-embolisation syndrome (low-grade fever, right upper quadrant pain, fatigue) is expected and resolves within 5-10 days.
complications: Spontaneous haemorrhage or rupture — occurs in fewer than 1% of all cases; risk is greatest in hemangiomas over 10 cm; presents as sudden severe abdominal pain with haemodynamic instability and is a surgical emergency.
Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon — consumptive coagulopathy with thrombocytopenia and haemolytic anaemia triggered by platelet trapping within a giant hemangioma; rare in adults, more common in children.
Biliary obstruction — a large hilar hemangioma can compress the common bile duct, causing jaundice and cholestasis; requires surgical decompression.
Diagnostic error — atypical or sclerosing hemangiomas may be misidentified as metastases on CT, leading to unnecessary biopsy, anxiety, or inappropriate oncological workup.
Post-embolisation syndrome — expected after transarterial embolisation; fever, pain, and elevated inflammatory markers lasting up to two weeks; rarely severe.

supportResources.name: American Liver Foundation — Benign Liver Tumours
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of benign liver tumours including hemangioma, with guidance on diagnosis and when to seek specialist care.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Liver Disease Information
supportResources.description: U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases patient hub covering liver conditions and when specialist referral is appropriate.

supportResources.name: NHS — Liver Haemangioma
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient information explaining what a liver haemangioma is, how it is diagnosed, and why treatment is rarely needed.

sources.title: Marrero JA et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: the Diagnosis and Management of Focal Liver Lesions. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 2014
Dietrich CF et al. EFSUMB Guidelines and Recommendations on the Clinical Use of Liver Ultrasound Elastography, Update 2017
Toro A et al. Indications and results of liver resection and transcatheter arterial chemoembolization for hepatic hemangiomas. HPB, 2014
European Association for the Study of the Liver (EASL). Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Benign Liver Tumours. Journal of Hepatology, 2016
Tap W et al. Hepatic hemangiomas: a review. Current Problems in Surgery, 2006
Schnelldorfer T et al. Experience with enucleation of symptomatic hepatic hemangiomas. Archives of Surgery, 2010

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatocellular carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatocellular carcinoma arises from hepatocytes and shows arterial enhancement with washout on CT/MRI; it elevates AFP and occurs predominantly in cirrhotic livers, whereas a hemangioma shows progressive fill-in, never washes out, and AFP is normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cholangiocarcinoma is a malignant bile duct tumour that often shows rim enhancement and progressive enhancement without complete fill-in; CA 19-9 may be elevated, unlike in hemangioma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatic angiosarcoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Angiosarcoma is a rare but aggressive malignant vascular tumour of the liver with heterogeneous imaging, rapid growth, and peritoneal spread — imaging characteristics overlap with giant hemangioma and biopsy is often required to distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Liver biopsy interpretation
relatedConditions.relevance: Biopsy with endothelial marker immunostaining is the definitive test for atypical hemangiomas that imaging cannot classify confidently.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis (general)
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic viral hepatitis creates a background of liver disease in which distinguishing a benign hemangioma from early hepatocellular carcinoma is a key diagnostic concern.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatic angiosarcoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Hepatic angiosarcoma is the principal malignant differential for large or atypical hepatic vascular lesions and must be excluded before a hemangioma is managed conservatively.

faqs.question: Is a liver hemangioma dangerous?
faqs.answer: For the vast majority of people, a liver hemangioma is entirely benign, causes no symptoms, and requires no treatment. It does not become cancerous, does not affect liver function, and does not shorten life expectancy. Fewer than 1% of cases experience serious complications.

faqs.question: Does a liver hemangioma need to be removed?
faqs.answer: Removal is rarely needed. Surgery is reserved for hemangiomas causing persistent disabling symptoms, those growing rapidly, lesions that cannot be confidently distinguished from a tumour after full imaging, or the very rare case of rupture. Over 90% of hemangiomas are managed with observation only.

faqs.question: Can a liver hemangioma turn into cancer?
faqs.answer: No. A liver hemangioma has no malignant potential and cannot transform into cancer. It is a benign vascular malformation, not a tumour. However, it can sometimes look like liver cancer on imaging, which is why confirmatory MRI or contrast-enhanced ultrasound is recommended to ensure the diagnosis is correct.

faqs.question: How is a liver hemangioma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Most are found incidentally on abdominal ultrasound. The diagnosis is confirmed with contrast-enhanced MRI, which shows a characteristic peripheral nodular enhancement with progressive fill-in and achieves over 95% accuracy. Biopsy is rarely needed and generally avoided because of the small bleeding risk.

faqs.question: Does a liver hemangioma cause pain?
faqs.answer: The majority cause no pain. Larger lesions, particularly those over 4-5 cm, can cause a dull right upper quadrant ache, fullness, or early satiety by pressing on nearby organs. Sudden severe pain is rare and warrants urgent medical assessment for haemorrhage.

faqs.question: Can liver hemangioma grow?
faqs.answer: Most hemangiomas remain stable in size for life. Fewer than 10-20% enlarge over time. Oestrogen promotes growth, so lesions are more likely to enlarge in women using oral contraceptives, HRT, or during pregnancy. Withdrawing oestrogen usually stabilises the lesion.

faqs.question: Should I stop taking the pill if I have a liver hemangioma?
faqs.answer: Discuss this with your clinician. For small hemangiomas under 3-4 cm, most guidelines do not require stopping combined oral contraceptives. For larger or growing lesions, switching to a progesterone-only or non-hormonal method is generally recommended, as oestrogen can promote further growth.

faqs.question: Is a liver hemangioma hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most hemangiomas are sporadic with no clear genetic inheritance. However, people with hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT, or Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome) can develop multiple hepatic vascular malformations including hemangiomas as part of that condition, which is autosomal dominant.

faqs.question: How common is a liver hemangioma?
faqs.answer: Liver hemangiomas are found in approximately 1-5% of people on imaging studies. Autopsy series suggest they may be present in up to 7% of the general population. They are the most common benign liver tumour and are far more frequent than liver cancer.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with a liver hemangioma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular moderate exercise is safe for virtually all people with liver hemangiomas. Those with giant lesions over 10 cm are sometimes advised to avoid high-impact contact sports that risk abdominal trauma, but swimming, walking, cycling, and gym exercise carry no meaningful risk.

faqs.question: Do I need regular scans for a liver hemangioma?
faqs.answer: For a typical hemangioma confirmed as benign on MRI or contrast-enhanced ultrasound, most guidelines recommend no further routine surveillance. Follow-up imaging is only needed if new symptoms develop, the lesion was not fully characterised initially, or the patient has a known reason for increased growth risk such as pregnancy.

faqs.question: Can a liver hemangioma bleed or rupture?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous rupture occurs in fewer than 1% of cases and is strongly associated with lesions larger than 10 cm or abdominal trauma. It presents as sudden severe abdominal pain with signs of bleeding and is a surgical emergency. The vast majority of hemangiomas never bleed.

faqs.question: Will pregnancy make my liver hemangioma grow?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy increases oestrogen levels and can cause some hemangiomas to enlarge, most commonly in the third trimester. Most growth is modest and does not cause problems, but women with large hemangiomas (over 5 cm) should have hepatology input and monitoring during pregnancy to identify the small group who develop symptoms.

faqs.question: What does a liver hemangioma look like on ultrasound?
faqs.answer: On standard ultrasound, a hemangioma typically appears as a well-defined, uniformly bright (hyperechoic) lesion with clear borders. However, this appearance is not specific enough to confirm the diagnosis on its own, and MRI or contrast-enhanced ultrasound is recommended to verify the lesion's nature.

faqs.question: Can I drink alcohol with a liver hemangioma?
faqs.answer: Moderate alcohol consumption does not directly cause hemangioma growth or rupture. However, alcohol damages liver tissue generally, and a healthy liver is important if surgery or intervention is ever needed. No specific alcohol restriction applies to hemangioma alone.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a hemangioma and liver cyst?
faqs.answer: A liver cyst is a fluid-filled sac with no blood flow, appearing black on ultrasound and showing no enhancement with contrast. A hemangioma is a blood-filled vascular malformation that shows characteristic enhancement on contrast studies. Both are benign, but they are distinct entities and are reliably distinguished on imaging.

faqs.question: What specialist should I see for a liver hemangioma?
faqs.answer: For an uncomplicated incidental hemangioma confirmed on imaging, a gastroenterologist or hepatologist can provide reassurance and follow-up guidance. For giant or symptomatic hemangiomas being considered for treatment, a hepatobiliary surgeon and an interventional radiologist should both be involved in the decision.

faqs.question: Does a liver hemangioma affect liver blood tests?
faqs.answer: No. Liver function tests (ALT, AST, bilirubin, albumin) and tumour markers (AFP, CEA, CA 19-9) are all normal in an isolated hemangioma. Abnormal blood tests suggest a separate liver problem and should be investigated independently.

faqs.question: Can children get liver hemangiomas?
faqs.answer: Infantile hepatic hemangiomas are a distinct entity seen in neonates and infants. They are the most common liver tumour in children under one year, often multiple, and many regress spontaneously by age two. A rare complication is Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon, a serious coagulopathy that requires active treatment. Adult cavernous hemangiomas are a separate lesion.

faqs.question: Is a liver hemangioma the same as a liver tumour?
faqs.answer: The word tumour simply means a mass or swelling and does not imply cancer. A liver hemangioma is classified as a benign tumour of vascular origin. It is not cancer, does not spread, and does not become malignant. The distinction from malignant liver tumours is confirmed by imaging.

metaTitle: Liver Hemangioma: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: A liver hemangioma is a benign blood-vessel mass found in 1-5% of people on imaging. Learn when it needs treatment and when it can be safely monitored.
